title: Wang et al. (2021) use a data-driven scientometric methodology to analyze a selected 815 articles in the field of energy…

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Wang et al. (2021) use a data-driven scientometric methodology to analyze a selected 815 articles in the field of energy policies associated with green finance. A clear rising trend is found throughout the related publications and citations. From the literature, they find four strands of policies, e.g., (i) green investments, (ii) green financing instruments, including green bonds, (iii) government subsidies, and (iv) emissions taxes, with the most attention to green bonds to support great future possibilities.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: The authors further identify the main actors in this field, including government agencies, international organizations, financial institutions, and research institutions. They also observe that most of the publications are from China, followed by the United States and European countries. The study concludes that the development of green finance policies is becoming a global trend, with increasing attention paid to sustainable finance and environmental protection.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: While a critical role in making green policies is suggested for policymakers and governments, firms are considered actual units to deliver production, innovation, and investment. Therefore, it is important to distinguish their motives and how decisions are designed when making effective policies (Zhang et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2021a). Studies on this issue can also be enhanced with blockchain technology, big data, and fintech. In the strand of firm-level green finance, Chevallier et al. (2021) focus on the latest global activities against climate change. While more economies commit to capturing carbon neutrality up to 2050, the policies related to energy transition have set off even more urgent. Notably, switching to a non-carbon-based fuel portfolio causes considerable risks met by firms in the energy sector. They use a stochastic model to simulate the effects of carbon-based assets and show that 17 oil-gas-coal firms are vulnerable to related financial risks.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The authors suggest that these firms need to transition their business models to adapt to the new energy landscape. Chevallier et al. (2021) also highlight the importance of green financing and investment in this transition. They argue that market instruments, such as green bonds and carbon pricing, can play a crucial role in mitigating financial risks and supporting the transition to green energy. 

Furthermore, the application of blockchain technology in the green finance sector has been explored in recent studies (Zhang et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2021a). Blockchain technology has the potential to provide a secure and transparent platform for green finance transactions, thus promoting the credibility and efficiency of this emerging industry. The use of big data and fintech can also assist in the development of green finance products and services. For example, big data analytics can aid in the assessment of environmental risks and the identification of green investment opportunities.

In summary, effective green finance policies require the collaboration of various stakeholders, including governments, financial institutions, and firms. The use of advanced technologies, such as blockchain, big data, and fintech, can assist in the development and implementation of these policies.
